skill.name: develop-userscripts
skill.description: Guides building, debugging, packaging, and publishing browser userscripts for Tampermonkey and ScriptCat, including metadata, GM APIs, permissions, ScriptCat background scripts, UserConfig, and subscriptions.
skill.summary: Build safer Tampermonkey and ScriptCat userscripts with correct metadata, permissions, APIs, and packaging choices.
skill.author: xixu-me

security_audit.summary: AI review did not confirm malicious intent or prompt injection. Most static findings are false positives caused by Markdown inline code, metadata tokens, URLs, and example terminology, but the skill legitimately guides userscript APIs that can access web pages, cross-origin requests, cookies, and cloud export features.

medium_findings.title: Userscript Network and Cookie Capability Surface
medium_findings.description: The skill legitimately covers GM_xmlhttpRequest, @connect, GM_cookie behavior, and ScriptCat cloud export features. These APIs are useful for userscripts, but generated scripts can become risky if prompts request broad hosts, cookie handling, or remote transfer of page data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly discuss cross-origin request APIs, special request headers, and exported cookies. The context is legitimate developer guidance, so this is a medium publication warning rather than evidence of malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Silent Subscription Update Behavior Requires Review
medium_findings.description: The skill documents ScriptCat subscription packages that can install multiple scripts and receive later silent updates unless permissions change. This is expected ScriptCat behavior, but marketplace users should review update URLs and subscription permissions carefully.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines explicitly describe subscription packaging, install links, and update behavior. The risk depends on how generated scripts are distributed, so the concern is real but not a confirmed abuse pattern.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The many Ruby or shell backtick findings are false positives. The backticks are Markdown formatting around userscript metadata and API names, not executable shell commands in skill logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text is Markdown documentation and tables, not a runnable Ruby or shell file. No evidence found of command execution instructions in the skill implementation.

low_findings.title: Documentation URLs Misclassified as Suspicious Network Activity
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URL findings are documentation links and illustrative example endpoints. They do not cause the skill to make network requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in markdown references or sample metadata. No evidence found that the skill itself fetches those URLs or exfiltrates data.

low_findings.title: Credential, Crypto, Filesystem, and Reconnaissance Keyword Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged words such as key, cookie, docs/dev, background.html, and metadata terms as sensitive files, weak crypto, device access, or reconnaissance. In context, they are userscript documentation terms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious tokens occur in explanatory prose, not in code that reads files, performs cryptography, or probes a system. The content still mentions cookies, so related generated scripts deserve review.

low_findings.title: Critical Heuristic Combination Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The combined static heuristic for code execution, network, and credential access is not confirmed. The scanned files are documentation for writing userscripts and contain no executable tool code or prompt injection attempt.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Full-file context shows markdown guidance and examples rather than active execution paths. No evidence found of instructions that override audit rules, hide behavior, or send secrets elsewhere.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad Userscript Permissions Can Be Generated If Prompted
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill discusses @grant, @connect, background scripts, and subscription-level permissions. These are valid userscript mechanisms, but users should avoid broad host or API permissions unless needed.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited guidance directly addresses permissions and connection scope. The skill often recommends minimizing permissions, so this is a dangerous capability pattern rather than a malicious pattern.

content.value_statement: Browser userscripts often fail because permissions, metadata, and runtime behavior are unclear. This skill guides Tampermonkey and ScriptCat work with focused patterns and checks.
actual_capabilities: Selects between portable foreground scripts, ScriptCat background scripts, cron scripts, and subscription packages.
Maps common userscript needs to metadata keys such as @match, @grant, @connect, @run-at, and update URLs.
Explains GM API usage for storage, menu commands, notifications, clipboard actions, tabs, resources, and cross-origin requests.
Guides ScriptCat-specific features including UserConfig blocks, background logging, CATRetryError, and CloudCat caveats.
Highlights common debugging points for manager logs, page consoles, background.html, and Manifest V3 browser requirements.
limitations: It is guidance only and does not execute, test, install, or publish userscripts by itself.
It does not replace browser, manager, or target-site security review before installing a script.
ScriptCat and Tampermonkey behavior can change, so current upstream documentation should be checked for releases.
It does not cover full browser extension development or unrelated browser automation frameworks.

use_cases.title: Create a portable page script
use_cases.description: Plan metadata, grants, and runtime timing for a script that modifies a specific website page.

use_cases.title: Debug ScriptCat background work
use_cases.description: Diagnose background script completion, retry behavior, logs, and Manifest V3 setup problems.

use_cases.title: Package a script subscription
use_cases.description: Prepare a ScriptCat subscription bundle with update metadata, script URLs, and connection permissions.

prompt_templates.title: Start a userscript
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for one target website.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a Tampermonkey userscript for this site. Identify the metadata block, grants, match rules, and safe defaults first.

prompt_templates.title: Fix missing permissions
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this userscript behavior and explain which @grant and @connect entries are missing or too broad.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a ScriptCat runtime
prompt_templates.prompt: Decide whether this task should be a foreground script, ScriptCat background script, cron script, or subscription package. Explain the tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a release package
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my ScriptCat subscription plan before release. Check update URLs, script URLs, connect permissions, UserConfig, and CloudCat constraints.

output_examples.input: I need a script that calls an API from a matched site.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends a foreground userscript, explicit site matching, a narrow connection host, and only the GM APIs required.

output_examples.input: My ScriptCat cron task finishes before storage writes complete.
output_examples.output: The assistant explains that background work should return a promise and settle only after asynchronous GM operations finish.

output_examples.input: I want to ship several scripts as one install link.
output_examples.output: The assistant suggests a UserSubscribe package, HTTPS install links, versioned updates, and careful subscription-level permissions.

best_practices: Start with metadata and permissions before writing page logic.
Use the smallest practical @match, @grant, and @connect scope.
Debug in the same runtime where the userscript will actually run.
anti_patterns: Do not request broad host access unless the use case requires it.
Do not use DOM APIs inside ScriptCat background or cron scripts.
Do not package subscriptions without reviewing update and connection behavior.

faq.question: Does this skill write complete userscripts?
faq.answer: It can guide and draft userscript structure, but the user must test and review the final script.

faq.question: Does it support Tampermonkey and ScriptCat?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers portable Tampermonkey patterns and ScriptCat-specific features where needed.

faq.question: Can it help with cross-origin requests?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains GM_xmlhttpRequest usage and the required @connect permission design.

faq.question: Can it create browser extensions?
faq.answer: No. It is focused on userscript managers, not full browser extension projects.

faq.question: Is it safe to use generated userscripts directly?
faq.answer: No. Userscripts can access pages and network APIs, so review permissions before installing them.

faq.question: Does it cover ScriptCat cloud execution?
faq.answer: It covers CloudCat caveats, exported values, exported cookies, and the reduced cloud API surface.
